lines.greek: ὦ πάτερ ἡμέτερε Κρονίδη, ὕπατε κρειόντων,
εἰ μὲν δὴ νῦν τοῦτο φίλον μακάρεσσι θεοῖσιν,
νοστῆσαι Ὀδυσῆα πολύφρονα ὅνδε δόμονδε,
Ἑρμείαν μὲν ἔπειτα διάκτορον ἀργεϊφόντην
νῆσον ἐς Ὠγυγίην ὀτρύνομεν, ὄφρα τάχιστα
νύμφῃ ἐυπλοκάμῳ εἴπῃ νημερτέα βουλήν,
νόστον Ὀδυσσῆος ταλασίφρονος, ὥς κε νέηται·
αὐτὰρ ἐγὼν Ἰθάκηνδʼ ἐσελεύσομαι, ὄφρα οἱ υἱὸν
μᾶλλον ἐποτρύνω καί οἱ μένος ἐν φρεσὶ θείω,
εἰς ἀγορὴν καλέσαντα κάρη κομόωντας Ἀχαιοὺς
πᾶσι μνηστήρεσσιν ἀπειπέμεν, οἵ τέ οἱ αἰεὶ
μῆλʼ ἁδινὰ σφάζουσι καὶ εἰλίποδας ἕλικας βοῦς.
πέμψω δʼ ἐς Σπάρτην τε καὶ ἐς Πύλον ἠμαθόεντα
νόστον πευσόμενον πατρὸς φίλου, ἤν που ἀκούσῃ,
ἠδʼ ἵνα μιν κλέος ἐσθλὸν ἐν ἀνθρώποισιν ἔχῃσιν.
ὣς εἰποῦσʼ ὑπὸ ποσσὶν ἐδήσατο καλὰ πέδιλα,
ἀμβρόσια χρύσεια, τά μιν φέρον ἠμὲν ἐφʼ ὑγρὴν
ἠδʼ ἐπʼ ἀπείρονα γαῖαν ἅμα πνοιῇς ἀνέμοιο·
εἵλετο δʼ ἄλκιμον ἔγχος, ἀκαχμένον ὀξέι χαλκῷ,
βριθὺ μέγα στιβαρόν, τῷ δάμνησι στίχας ἀνδρῶν

interlinear: 'O father our, son-of-Cronos, highest of-rulers,
if indeed now this dear-is to-the-blessed gods,
that-return Odysseus the-wise to-his-own home,
Hermes then the-guide, the-Argeiphontes,
island to Ogygia let-us-send, so-that most-swiftly
to-the-nymph fair-tressed he-tell the-unerring counsel,
the-return of-Odysseus steadfast-hearted, that he-may-come-home.
but I to-Ithaca will-go, so-that his son
the-more I-rouse and in-him might in his-heart I-set,
to an-assembly having-called the-long-haired Achaeans,
to-all the-wooers to-renounce, who for-him ever
sheep thronging slaughter and rolling-gaited crook-horned oxen.
and-I-will-send to Sparta and to Pylos sandy
of-the-homecoming to-inquire of-his-father dear, if-somewhere he-hear,
and that him fame noble among men may-have.
so having-spoken, beneath her-feet she-bound fair sandals,
ambrosial, golden, which her bore both over the-wet
and over the-boundless earth with the-breaths of-wind.
and-she-took a-valiant spear, pointed with-sharp bronze,
heavy, great, sturdy, with-which she-subdues ranks of-men,
literal: 'Our Father, son of Cronos, highest of lords,
if indeed this is now the pleasure of the blessed gods,
that wise Odysseus should return to his own home,
then let us send Hermes, the guide, the Argeiphontes,
to the island of Ogygia, that with all speed
he may tell the fair-tressed nymph our unerring resolve—
the homecoming of steadfast-hearted Odysseus, that he may return.
But I will go to Ithaca, so that I may rouse his son
the more, and set courage in his heart,
to call the long-haired Achaeans to an assembly
and speak out against all the wooers, who forever
slaughter his thronging sheep and his shambling, crook-horned oxen.
And I will send him to Sparta and to sandy Pylos
to seek news of his dear father's return, if he should hear of it,
so that noble fame may be his among men.'
So she spoke, and bound beneath her feet her fair sandals,
immortal, golden, which bore her both over the water
and over the boundless earth with the breath of the wind.
And she took her mighty spear, tipped with sharp bronze,
heavy and huge and strong, with which she subdues the ranks of men,
translations.elizabethan:
"...our Father, son of Cronos, lord most high:
if now it be the pleasure of the blest
that wise Odysseus come again to home,
then let us send the guide, keen Hermes, down
to far Ogygia's isle, that with all speed
he tell the fair-tressed nymph our fixed decree—
the steadfast man's return, that he may come.
And I to Ithaca will go, to rouse
his son the more, and set within his breast
fresh courage, that he call to council-ground
the long-haired Achaeans, and denounce
the suitors all, who ever slay his flocks,
his thronging sheep, his shambling crook-horned kine.
To Sparta then and sandy Pylos' shore
I'll send him, news to seek of his lov'd sire,
if word there be—and so win noble fame
amongst mankind." She spoke, and 'neath her feet
bound her fair sandals, deathless, wrought of gold,
that bear her over sea and boundless land
swift as the wind's own breath. Her spear she took,
stout, tipped with biting bronze, huge, heavy, strong,
wherewith she lays the ranks of heroes low—
translations.modernist:
"Our Father, son of Cronos, highest of the lords. If the blessed gods want this now—for wise Odysseus to go home—then let us send Hermes, the guide, to the island of Ogygia. Let him tell the nymph with the lovely hair what we have decided. That the steadfast man goes home. As for me, I will go to Ithaca. I will stir up his son. I will put courage in him. He will call the long-haired Achaeans to an assembly and speak out against the suitors. They are always killing his sheep and his cattle. And I will send him to Sparta and to sandy Pylos, to look for news of his father, in case he hears something. He will get a good name among men."

She finished. She tied her sandals on—beautiful, golden, everlasting. They carried her over water and over the whole wide earth, fast as the wind. She took her spear. Bronze at the tip, sharp. Heavy, huge, strong. She breaks the ranks of men with it—
translations.storybook:
"Father Zeus, highest of all the gods, if the blessed gods have truly decided it is time for clever Odysseus to go home at last, then here is my plan. Let us send Hermes, our messenger, all the way to the faraway island of Ogygia, to tell the nymph with the beautiful hair that we have made up our minds: brave Odysseus is going home. And I myself will slip down to Ithaca, to give his son a little push and put some courage in his heart. I'll have him call all the long-haired Achaeans together and tell those greedy suitors to leave—for they do nothing but gobble up his sheep and his fine cattle, day after day! Then I'll send him off to Sparta and to sandy Pylos, to see if he can find any news of his dear father. And that way he'll win a fine name for himself among people everywhere."

And with that, she strapped her lovely sandals onto her feet—golden ones, that never wear out, which carry her over the sea and over the whole wide world as fast as the wind. Then she picked up her spear, the one tipped with sharp bronze—heavy and huge and strong—the very spear she uses to knock down whole rows of soldiers—

murray.text: to the isle Ogygia, that with all speed he may declare to the fair-tressed nymph our fixed resolve, even the return of Odysseus of the steadfast heart, that he may come home. But, as for me, I will go to Ithaca, that I may the more arouse his son, and set courage in his heart

to call to an assembly the long-haired Achaeans, and speak out his word to all the wooers, who are ever slaying his thronging sheep and his sleek kine of shambling gait. And I will guide him to Sparta and to sandy Pylos, to seek tidings of the return of his dear father, if haply he may hear of it,

that good report may be his among men.” So she spoke, and bound beneath her feet her beautiful sandals, immortal, golden, which were wont to bear her both over the waters of the sea and over the boundless land swift as the blasts of the wind. And she took her mighty spear, tipped with sharp bronze,

heavy and huge and strong, wherewith she vanquishes the ranks of men—of warriors, with whom she is wroth, she, the daughter of the mighty sire. Then she went darting down from the heights of Olympus, and took her stand in the land of Ithaca at the outer gate of Odysseus, on the threshold of the court. In her hand she held the spear of bronze,
